P1: A franchise celebrated its 20th anniversary. At the time of the franchise's debut, one individual's age was exactly twice another individual's age. If the older individual is currently 28 years old, what is the current age of the younger individual?
P2: So you know how Digimon turned 20? Well, back when it launched, John's age was double Jim's. Now John's 28—any idea how old Jim is?
P3: The 20th anniversary of Digimon was recently celebrated. At the time of its release, Jim's age was half of John's age. If John is now 28 years old, how old is Jim currently?
P4: Digimon had its 20th anniversary. When it debuted, one person was twice the age of another. If the older person has now reached an age that is four less than thirty-two, how old is the younger person today?
P5: A famous video game franchise celebrated its 20th anniversary. When the game was first released, Marcus was twice as old as Elena. If Marcus is 28 years old now, how old is Elena?

P1: Let C, D, and J represent the number of friends belonging to Charlie, Dorothy, and James respectively. Given that C = 3D and J = 4D, and C = 12, what is the value of J?
P2: On Facebook, Charlie's friend list is triple Dorothy's and James has quadruple Dorothy's count. Given that Charlie has 12 friends, what's James's friend count?
P3: Four times as many Facebook friends as Dorothy are had by James. Three times as many as Dorothy are had by Charlie. If 12 friends are had by Charlie, how many Facebook friends are had by James?
P4: Charlie's Facebook friend count is triple Dorothy's, while James has one more times Dorothy's count than Charlie does. If Charlie has a dozen friends on Facebook, how many friends does James have?
P5: A farm has three animal pens. The chicken pen holds three times as many animals as the duck pen, and the goose pen holds four times as many as the duck pen. If there are 12 chickens, how many geese are there?

P1: Given that each of 10 dogs requires 0.5 hours of daily care, compute the total weekly hours spent on dog care?
P2: John looks after 10 dogs, and each one needs half an hour a day for walks and stuff. How many hours a week does he spend on them?
P3: Half an hour per day is needed to walk and care for each dog. If 10 dogs are being taken care of by John, how many hours per week are spent on dog care?
P4: John takes care of a number of dogs equal to twice five. Each dog requires a fraction of an hour equal to one-half for daily walking and care. How many hours does he spend per week on the dogs?
P5: Maria tends to 10 flower beds in her garden. Each flower bed takes 0.5 hours a day to water and weed. How many hours a week does she spend tending her garden?

P1: Let I = 6 represent the number of letters in Indra's name. Her sister's name contains (I/2) + 4 letters. Compute the sum I + ((I/2) + 4)?
P2: Indra's name has 6 letters. Her sister's name has 4 more letters than half of Indra's. How many letters do both names have combined?
P3: Six letters are contained in Indra's name. Four more letters than half the number of letters in Indra's name are found in her sister's name. How many letters are contained in both names together?
P4: Indra's name has a number of letters equal to half a dozen. Her sister's name has a letter count that exceeds half of Indra's letter count by four. What is the total number of letters across both names?
P5: A small box holds 6 apples. A large box holds 4 more apples than half the number in the small box. How many apples are in both boxes combined?

P1: Four individuals possess 8, 10, 1, and 5 items respectively. If the total items are distributed equally among the four, how many items does each individual receive?
P2: DJ has 8 paintball guns, Nick has 10, RJ has 1, and Richard has 5. If they split them all equally, how many would each person get?
P3: Eight guns are owned by DJ, ten by Nick, one by RJ, and five by Richard. If all guns were shared equally among the four of them, how many guns would be received by each person?
P4: Among four friends with paintball guns, one has eight, another has two more than that first person, a third has just a single gun, and the last has half of what the person with ten has. If they pool and divide equally, how many guns does each person end up with?
P5: Four farmers bring produce to a market. Farmer A brings 8 baskets, Farmer B brings 10, Farmer C brings 1, and Farmer D brings 5. If they redistribute all baskets equally, how many baskets does each farmer get?

P1: A group of 105 members is divided into three subgroups: offense, defense, and special teams. The offense has twice the membership of the defense, and special teams has half the membership of the defense. How many members are on the defense?
P2: A football team has 105 players total. There are twice as many on offense as defense, and special teams has half as many as defense. How many are on defense?
P3: One hundred and five members make up a football team. Twice as many players as are on defense are placed on offense, and half as many as are on defense are assigned to special teams. How many players are on the defense?
P4: A football team's roster totals a number five more than one hundred. The offensive unit is double the size of the defensive unit, while the special teams unit is half the defensive unit's size. How many players make up the defense?
P5: A company has 105 employees split across three departments: sales, support, and administration. Sales has twice as many staff as support, and administration has half as many as support. How many employees work in support?

P1: Each duck in a flock of 10 requires 3.5 pounds of insects per week for survival. Determine the daily insect consumption for the entire flock in pounds?
P2: So each duck has to munch through 3.5 pounds of bugs every week just to get by. With a group of ten ducks, what's their daily bug intake in pounds?
P3: 3.5 pounds of insects per week are required by each duck for survival. If a flock of ten ducks is considered, how many pounds of insects are needed per day?
P4: Each duck in a flock needs to consume half of seven pounds of insects weekly. If the flock size is a full ten, how many pounds of insects does the flock require daily?
P5: Each machine in a factory consumes 3.5 gallons of oil per week to operate. If there are ten machines running, how many gallons of oil are needed per day?

P1: Given that the number of pink gumballs equals 4 times the number of blue gumballs plus 22, and there are 12 blue gumballs, calculate the number of pink gumballs?
P2: Picture a gumball machine where the pink ones outnumber four times the blue ones by 22. With 12 blue gumballs inside, what's the pink gumball count?
P3: Twelve blue gumballs are found in a candy machine. The number of pink gumballs is determined to be 22 more than four times the blue ones. How many pink gumballs are contained in the machine?
P4: A candy machine has blue gumballs numbering a dozen. The pink gumballs exceed four times the blue count by twenty-two. How many pink gumballs does the machine contain?
P5: In a garden, there are 22 more than four times as many roses as there are tulips. If there are 12 tulips, how many roses are in the garden?

P1: Two adults and one child attend the cinema. Adult tickets cost $12 each and child tickets cost $8. They also purchase 2 popcorns at $3 each. Compute the total expenditure?
P2: A couple and their kid go to the movies. Adult tickets are $12 each and a kid's ticket is $8. They also grab 2 popcorns for $3 each. How much do they spend altogether?
P3: The cinema is visited by two parents and a child. $12 is charged per adult ticket and $8 per child ticket. Two popcorns are purchased at $3 each. How much is paid in total?
P4: A family of two adults and one child visits the cinema, where each adult ticket costs a dozen dollars and the child ticket costs two-thirds of an adult ticket. They also buy a pair of popcorns at $3 apiece. What is the total amount they pay?
P5: Two adults and one child visit an amusement park. Adult entry costs $12 and child entry costs $8. They also buy 2 drinks at $3 each. How much do they spend in total?

P1: Paper stacked at a density of 100 pages per inch is printed on both sides with one page per side. For a stack thickness of 1.5 inches, determine the total number of pages in the book?
P2: A book is printed on paper where you can stack 100 pages per inch, and each sheet has a page on the front and back. If the book is 1.5 inches thick, how many pages does it have?
P3: Both sides of each sheet of paper are printed with one page per side. When stacked, the paper measures 100 pages per inch. If 1.5 inches of thickness are measured, how many pages are contained in the book?
P4: A book's paper, when stacked, has a density of a hundred pages per inch, with each physical sheet bearing a page on both its front and back. If the book's thickness is one and a half inches, how many pages does the book contain?
P5: Bricks are stacked at a rate of 100 per foot of height, and each brick has a design stamped on both its top and bottom face. If a wall of bricks is 1.5 feet tall, how many designs are on the bricks in total?

P1: Person A made 50 acquaintances. Person B made 20 more acquaintances than Person A. What is the combined total of acquaintances made by both persons?
P2: When Amy switched to a new school, she ended up making 20 more friends than her pal Lily, who made 50. What's their combined friend count?
P3: Fifty friends were made by Lily. Twenty more friends than Lily were made by Amy after transferring. How many friends are had by Lily and Amy combined?
P4: Lily made a number of friends equal to half a hundred. Amy made a count that exceeds Lily's by a score. How many friends do the two of them have in total?
P5: After joining a new gym, Carlos gained 20 more workout partners than Diana. If Diana gained 50 workout partners, how many workout partners do Carlos and Diana have altogether?

P1: Three large bags each weigh 10 ounces. Each ounce contains 30 units. The total units are to be distributed into small bags of 10 units each. How many small bags can be made?
P2: John buys 3 big bags of M&Ms weighing 10 ounces each. There are 30 M&Ms per ounce, and he puts 10 in each small bag. How many small bags can he make?
P3: Ten ounces are weighed by each of the 3 large bags purchased. Thirty M&Ms are found in each ounce. If 10 M&Ms are placed into each small bag, how many small bags can be filled?
P4: John purchases three large bags, each weighing ten ounces. Every ounce holds three dozen M&Ms. If each small bag is filled with a count equal to one-third of those in an ounce, how many small bags can he make?
P5: A factory receives 3 crates of bolts, each weighing 10 pounds. Each pound contains 30 bolts. If the bolts are repackaged into small boxes of 10 bolts each, how many small boxes can be filled?

P1: From a population of 50 deer, 50% are bucks. Of those bucks, 20% are 8-point bucks. Determine the number of 8-point bucks?
P2: There are 50 deer in a field. Half of them are bucks, and 20% of those bucks are 8-pointers. How many 8-point bucks are there?
P3: Fifty deer are found in a field. Bucks make up 50% of the deer, and 8-point bucks represent 20% of the bucks. How many 8-point bucks are there?
P4: In a field with half a hundred deer, half are bucks, and one-fifth of the bucks have eight points. How many 8-point bucks are present?
P5: A parking lot has 50 cars. Half of them are sedans, and 20% of those sedans are electric. How many electric sedans are in the lot?

P1: Starting with 30 items, 2 are consumed. The remaining items are packaged in groups of 2. How many complete groups can be formed?
P2: Jean has 30 lollipops and eats 2. She then puts the rest into bags of 2. How many bags can she fill?
P3: Two lollipops are eaten from a collection of 30. The remaining lollipops are to be placed 2 per bag. How many bags can be filled?
P4: Jean starts with three dozen minus six lollipops. After eating a pair of them, she bags the rest with two per bag. How many bags can she fill?
P5: A baker has 30 muffins and eats 2 for breakfast. He then packages the remaining muffins into boxes of 2. How many boxes can he fill?

P1: Let S, B, and R represent the ages of three phones. Given B = 2S and R = 4B, and S = 1, determine R?
P2: Brandon's iPhone is four times as old as Ben's, and Ben's is twice as old as Suzy's. If Suzy's phone is 1 year old, how old is Brandon's?
P3: Suzy's iPhone is 1 year old. Ben's iPhone is twice as old as Suzy's. Brandon's iPhone is four times as old as Ben's. How old is Brandon's iPhone?
P4: Brandon's phone has been around four times as long as Ben's, whose phone in turn has been owned for double the time Suzy has had hers. If Suzy got her phone just a single year ago, how old is Brandon's phone?
P5: A grandfather oak tree is four times as old as a parent oak, and the parent oak is twice as old as a sapling. If the sapling is 1 year old, how old is the grandfather oak?

P1: An individual's expenditure in month one was $500. In month two, the expenditure decreased by $60. Calculate the total expenditure over both months?
P2: Joseph spent $500 in May. In June, he spent $60 less. What was his total spending for both months?
P3: $500 was spent by Joseph in May. $60 less than that amount was spent in June. How much was spent in total over the two months?
P4: Joseph's May expenditure was five hundred dollars. His June spending was sixty dollars short of his May spending. What was his combined expenditure for both months?
P5: A factory produced 500 widgets in January. In February, production decreased by 60 widgets. How many widgets were produced in total over the two months?

P1: Rose purchased 4 times the quantity of onions and potatoes that Sophia purchased. Rose acquired 12 onions and 4 potatoes. Determine the total number of onions and potatoes Sophia purchased?
P2: Rose bought 4 times as many onions and potatoes as Sophia. If Rose got 12 onions and 4 potatoes, how many onions and potatoes did Sophia buy altogether?
P3: Twelve onions and four potatoes were bought by Rose. This quantity is four times what was bought by Sophia. How many onions and potatoes were purchased by Sophia in total?
P4: Rose's onion and potato purchase was quadruple Sophia's. If Rose ended up with a dozen onions and four potatoes, what is the total count of onions and potatoes that Sophia bought?
P5: At a craft fair, Lena bought 4 times as many beads and buttons as Mia. If Lena bought 12 beads and 4 buttons, how many beads and buttons did Mia buy in total?

P1: Individual A earns $0.80 per task and Individual B earns $0.50 per task. If each completes 20 tasks, compute the difference in their total earnings?
P2: Jairus gets $0.80 per small task and Jenny gets $0.50. If they each finish 20 tasks, how much more does Jairus earn than Jenny?
P3: $0.80 is earned by Jairus and $0.50 by Jenny for each small task completed. If 20 tasks are finished by each of them, by how much is Jairus's total earning greater than Jenny's?
P4: For each task, Jairus earns thirty cents more than Jenny, who earns half a dollar per task. If both complete a score of tasks, how much more does Jairus earn than Jenny?
P5: Machine A produces $0.80 worth of output per cycle and Machine B produces $0.50 per cycle. If both machines run 20 cycles, how much more output value does Machine A generate than Machine B?

P1: An individual consumes 4 units on each of 5 weekdays and 5 units on each of 2 weekend days. Calculate the total weekly consumption?
P2: Mason eats 4 carrots every weekday and 5 each on Saturday and Sunday. How many carrots does he eat in a week?
P3: Four carrots are eaten on each weekday and five on each weekend day. How many carrots are consumed by Mason in a week?
P4: Mason eats one fewer carrot on each weekday than he does on each weekend day, where his weekend consumption is five per day. How many carrots does he eat in a full week?
P5: A jogger runs 4 miles on each weekday and 5 miles on each day of the weekend. How many miles does the jogger run in a week?

P1: A customer pays $500 for materials and $800 for labor. An insurance fee of 10% of the combined material and labor cost is then added. Calculate the total amount paid?
P2: Janet pays $500 for materials and $800 for a jeweler to make a brooch. Then she pays 10% of that total to insure it. How much did she pay in all?
P3: $500 was paid for materials and $800 for construction of a brooch. An insurance cost of 10% of the combined amount was then paid. How much was paid in total?
P4: Janet pays five hundred dollars for materials and eight hundred for craftsmanship. She then insures the piece for a tenth of the combined cost of materials and labor. What is her total expenditure?
P5: A homeowner pays $500 for building materials and $800 for a contractor's labor to build a deck. She then pays 10% of the total construction cost for a warranty. How much does she pay altogether?

P1: A small entity contains 3 dozen units. A large entity contains 50% more units than the small entity. Compute the total number of units across both entities?
P2: A small sunflower has 3 dozen seeds and a big one has 50% more seeds than the small one. How many seeds are there in total?
P3: Three dozen seeds are contained in a small sunflower. Fifty percent more seeds than the small sunflower are found in a large sunflower. How many seeds are there altogether?
P4: A small sunflower has seeds numbering three times twelve. A large sunflower has half again as many seeds as the small one. What is the combined seed count of both sunflowers?
P5: A small jar holds 3 dozen marbles. A large jar holds 50% more marbles than the small jar. How many marbles are there in both jars combined?

P1: An individual may consume cookies equal to half the number of carrot sticks eaten plus 2 additional cookies. If the desired total is 5 cookies, determine the number of carrot sticks required?
P2: Matt's dad says he can have half as many cookies as carrot sticks he eats, plus 2 extra for cleaning his room. If Matt wants 5 cookies, how many carrot sticks does he need to eat?
P3: Two extra cookies are given for cleaning his room, and half as many cookies as carrot sticks eaten are allowed. If 5 cookies are desired by Matt in total, how many carrot sticks must be eaten?
P4: Matt is allowed cookies numbering half his carrot stick intake plus a bonus of two for tidying up. If he aims to enjoy a total of five cookies, how many carrot sticks must he consume?
P5: A student earns screen time equal to half the number of pages read plus 2 bonus minutes for organizing their desk. If the student wants 5 minutes of screen time, how many pages must they read?

P1: An individual requires 56 units. Units are sold in boxes of 8 at a cost of $2.00 per box. Calculate the total cost?
P2: Bob needs 56 popsicle sticks for a project. They come 8 to a box and each box costs $2. How much will he spend?
P3: Fifty-six popsicle sticks are needed by Bob. Eight sticks are included in each box, and $2.00 is charged per box. How much will be spent by Bob?
P4: Bob needs a number of popsicle sticks equal to seven times eight. Each box holds eight sticks and costs two dollars. How much must he spend on his project?
P5: A teacher needs 56 colored pencils for an art class. Pencils come in packs of 8, each costing $2.00. How much will the teacher spend?

P1: An individual reported an attendance figure of 48, which represented an overstatement of 20% above the actual count. Determine the true attendance?
P2: Courtney said 48 people were at the concert, but Kelly says she overcounted by 20%. If Kelly's right, how many people actually attended?
P3: An attendance of 48 was reported by Courtney, but this figure was overstated by 20% according to Kelly. How many people actually attended the concert?
P4: Courtney claimed the audience numbered forty-eight, but this was found to be a fifth more than the real count. What was the actual number of attendees?
P5: A student estimated that a jar contained 48 marbles, but a teacher determined the student had overestimated by 20%. How many marbles were actually in the jar?

P1: Starting with $500, 20% is spent initially. Then 25% of the remaining amount is spent. Determine the final remaining amount?
P2: Julie had $500. She blew 20% on clothes, then 25% of what was left on CDs. How much money does she have left?
P3: $500 was had by Julie. Twenty percent was spent on clothes. Then 25% of the remaining amount was spent on CDs. How much money was left?
P4: Julie started with five hundred dollars. She spent a fifth of it on clothes, then a quarter of the remainder on CDs. How much did she have left?
P5: A water tank holds 500 liters. First, 20% of the water is drained. Then 25% of the remaining water is used. How many liters of water remain in the tank?

P1: On day one, 4 items are collected. On day two, 6 items are collected. On day three, three times the day-one quantity is collected. Compute the total items collected?
P2: John picks 4 bananas on Wednesday, 6 on Thursday, and triple what he picked on Wednesday on Friday. How many bananas does he have total?
P3: Four bananas are picked on Wednesday and six on Thursday. On Friday, triple the number picked on Wednesday is collected. How many bananas have been picked in total?
P4: John picks some bananas on Wednesday. On Thursday he picks two more than he did on Wednesday, and on Friday he triples his Wednesday haul. If he picked four on Wednesday, how many bananas does he have altogether?
P5: A factory produces 4 widgets on Monday, 6 on Tuesday, and three times Monday's output on Wednesday. How many widgets were produced in total?

P1: A document of 15 pages must be completed. One-third of it has already been written. How many pages remain to be written?
P2: Shiela needs to write a 15-page paper and she's already done a third of it. How many pages does she still have to write?
P3: A 15-page research paper is required to be submitted by Shiela. One-third of the paper has already been finished. How many pages are left to be written?
P4: Shiela must submit a research paper whose page count is three times five. Having already completed a third of it, how many pages remain for her to write?
P5: A builder must lay 15 rows of bricks for a wall. He has already completed one-third of the rows. How many rows does he still need to lay?

P1: On a 60-mile journey, the first stop occurs at mile 20. The second stop occurs 15 miles before the endpoint. Calculate the distance between the two stops?
P2: Henry biked 60 miles total. He stopped first after 20 miles and again 15 miles before the end. How far did he ride between his two stops?
P3: A 60-mile bike trip was taken by Henry. The first stop was made after 20 miles, and the second stop was made 15 miles before the end. How many miles were traveled between the two stops?
P4: On a trip spanning three score miles, Henry made his first stop a third of the way in. His second stop was fifteen miles shy of the finish. How many miles separated his two stops?
P5: A hiker walks a 60-kilometer trail. She rests first at the 20-kilometer mark and again at a point 15 kilometers before the trail's end. How many kilometers did she walk between her two rest stops?

P1: An item costs $400 at market price. A DIY alternative saves 20% off the market price. Calculate the cost of the DIY option?
P2: A hand-painted wallpaper costs $400 at the store. Making it yourself saves 20%. How much does the DIY version cost?
P3: $400 is the market price for a hand-painted wallpaper. A savings of 20% is achieved by making it oneself. How much is spent on the DIY version?
P4: A hand-painted wallpaper's market price is four hundred dollars. By doing it yourself, you save a fifth of that price. What does the DIY wallpaper end up costing?
P5: A custom birthday cake costs $400 from a bakery. Baking it at home saves 20% compared to the bakery price. How much does the homemade cake cost?

P1: Supplies cost $400 in total. Travel tickets cost 50% more than the supplies. Determine the combined total cost of supplies and tickets?
P2: Jen's traveling to 3 countries. Supplies cost $400 total, and the tickets cost 50% more than the supplies. How much does the whole trip cost?
P3: $400 is paid for supplies. Travel tickets are priced at 50% more than the supplies total. How much does travel cost altogether?
P4: Jen pays four hundred dollars for supplies. Her tickets cost half again as much as her supplies. What is her total travel expenditure?
P5: A contractor pays $400 for raw materials. The labor cost is 50% more than the materials. What is the total project cost?

P1: Given 5 plants yielding 22 units each and 8 plants yielding 4 units each, compute the total yield?
P2: Ricardo planted 5 tomato plants that give 22 tomatoes each, and 8 eggplant plants that give 4 eggplants each. How many fruits does he get altogether?
P3: Twenty-two tomatoes are yielded by each of the 5 tomato plants, and 4 eggplants are produced by each of the 8 eggplant plants. How many fruits are obtained in total?
P4: Ricardo has five tomato plants, each producing twenty-two tomatoes, and twice as many eggplant plants minus two, each producing four eggplants. How many total fruits does he harvest?
P5: A factory has 5 assembly lines producing 22 units each and 8 packaging stations processing 4 units each. How many total units are handled?

P1: A person travels 210 miles (200 + 10 mile detour) at 70 mph, then returns via a 240-mile route at 80 mph. Calculate the total travel time in hours?
P2: John drives 200 miles to his friend's place at 70 mph but has to take a 10-mile detour. Coming back, he takes a 240-mile route at 80 mph. How long does the whole trip take?
P3: A 200-mile drive at 70 mph is taken, with a 10-mile detour added. The return trip covers 240 miles at 80 mph. How many hours does the entire trip take?
P4: John drives to his friend's house two hundred miles away at seventy miles per hour, but a detour adds ten extra miles. His return route is forty miles longer than the original distance, traveled at eighty miles per hour. How many hours does the total trip take?
P5: A delivery truck travels 210 miles (including a 10-mile detour) to a warehouse at 70 mph, then returns by a 240-mile route at 80 mph. How many hours does the round trip take?

P1: The daily recommended coffee intake is 4 cups. Individual A drinks half the recommendation. Individual B drinks 10 times Individual A's intake. Calculate how many cups Individual B must reduce to meet the recommendation?
P2: Octavia drinks half the recommended 4 cups of coffee a day. Her husband Juan drinks 10 times what she does. His doctor says cut back to 4 cups. How many cups does Juan need to cut?
P3: Half of the recommended 4 daily cups of coffee is consumed by Octavia. Ten times her intake is consumed by Juan. By how many cups must Juan's intake be reduced to reach the recommended 4 cups?
P4: Octavia consumes half the daily recommended amount of coffee, which is four cups. Juan drinks ten times Octavia's amount. If Juan's doctor wants him at the recommended level, by how many cups must he reduce his intake?
P5: The daily recommended water intake is 4 glasses. Mia drinks half the recommendation. Her brother Leo drinks 10 times what Mia drinks. How many glasses must Leo reduce to meet the 4-glass recommendation?

P1: A cyclist travels at a constant speed of 8 miles per hour. After riding for 4 hours, resting, and then riding for an additional 6 hours, what is the total distance traveled?
P2: Mason bikes at 8 miles per hour. He rides for 4 hours, takes a break, then rides for 6 more hours. How many miles does he cover in total?
P3: Eight miles per hour is the rate at which Mason travels on his bike. Four hours of riding are followed by rest, and then another six hours of riding. How many miles are traveled in total?
P4: Mason bikes at a speed of eight miles per hour. He first rides for a duration equal to two-thirds of six hours, rests, then continues for half again as long as his first ride. What total distance does he cover?
P5: A boat travels at a constant speed of 8 knots. It sails for 4 hours, anchors for a rest, and then sails for another 6 hours. How many nautical miles does it travel in total?

P1: Phase 1 takes 20 minutes. Phase 2 takes 40% longer than Phase 1. Phase 3 takes 5 minutes less than the sum of Phases 1 and 2. Compute the total time across all three phases?
P2: It takes 20 minutes to heat oil to 300 degrees. Getting it to 400 takes 40% longer than that first step. Then cooking takes 5 minutes less than the total heating time. How long from start to finish?
P3: Twenty minutes are taken for the oil to reach 300 degrees. Forty percent longer is required to reach 400 degrees. Five minutes less than the total warming time is needed for cooking. How much total time passes?
P4: Oil takes a third of an hour to reach 300 degrees. Reaching 400 degrees takes two-fifths longer than that initial heating. The cooking time is five minutes less than the combined heating time. What is the total time from start to cooked chicken?
P5: A printer takes 20 minutes to warm up. Calibration takes 40% longer than the warm-up. The actual print job then takes 5 minutes less than the combined warm-up and calibration time. How long does the entire process take from start to finish?

P1: An individual starts with 24 items. Person A takes 5, Person B takes twice what A took, Person C gives an unknown quantity, and Person D takes 3 fewer than A took. If the individual ends with 27 items, how many did Person C give?
P2: Griffin had 24 fries. Kyle took 5, Billy took twice that, Ginger gave him some, and Colby took 3 less than Kyle did. If Griffin ended up with 27 fries, how many did Ginger give him?
P3: Twenty-four french fries were had by Griffin. Five were taken by Kyle, and twice that number by Billy. Some fries were given to Griffin by Ginger. Three fewer than Kyle's amount were taken by Colby. If 27 fries were had by Griffin at the end, how many were given by Ginger?
P4: Griffin starts with two dozen fries. Kyle takes five, Billy takes double Kyle's amount, and Colby takes two fewer than Kyle. After Ginger contributes some fries, Griffin ends up with twenty-seven. How many fries did Ginger give Griffin?
P5: A warehouse starts with 24 crates. Truck A removes 5, Truck B removes twice what Truck A did, and Truck D removes 3 fewer than Truck A. A delivery adds an unknown number. If the warehouse ends with 27 crates, how many crates were delivered?

P1: Holes dug over three days total 10, 13, and 8 respectively, with 2 holes refilled on day three. By end of day four, total holes equal 4 times the day-one total. How many holes were dug on day four?
P2: Pirates dug 10 holes day one, 13 day two, and 8 day three but filled in 2 of them. By day four when they found treasure, the island had 4 times as many holes as after day one. How many did they dig on day four?
P3: Ten holes were dug the first day, thirteen the second, and eight the third, though two were filled in on the third day. By the fourth day, four times as many holes as existed after the first day were present. How many holes were dug on the fourth day?
P4: A crew digs ten holes the first day, three more than that the second day, and eight the third day while filling in a pair. By day four the total is quadruple the first day's count. How many holes were dug on day four?
P5: Workers planted 10 trees on Monday, 13 on Tuesday, and 8 on Wednesday but removed 2 that were damaged. By Thursday, the total number of trees was 4 times Monday's count. How many trees were planted on Thursday?

P1: Compute the total value in dollars of 100 pennies, 40 nickels, 20 dimes, and 40 one-dollar bills?
P2: Brady has 100 pennies, 40 nickels, 20 dimes, and 40 dollar bills in his piggy bank. How much money does he have in total?
P3: One hundred pennies, forty nickels, twenty dimes, and forty dollar bills are found in Brady's piggy bank. How much money is contained in the piggy bank in dollars?
P4: Brady's piggy bank contains a hundred pennies, two score nickels, a score of dimes, and forty dollar bills. What is the total value of the contents in dollars?
P5: A cashier counts the register and finds 100 one-cent stamps, 40 five-cent stamps, 20 ten-cent stamps, and 40 one-dollar stamps. What is the total value of all the stamps in dollars?
